intentional_test_features: Textbook renal-colic constellation (radiating unilateral flank pain, extreme restlessness, macroscopic haematuria) — the diagnostic test is easy; the real test is telehealth triage discipline and NOT missing the life-threatening mimic (leaking AAA) and the key complication (infected obstructed kidney / urosepsis).

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present after-hours clinician who administered IM ketorolac and IM/ODT ondansetron and performed abdominal palpation and CVA percussion; the AI Doctor CANNOT examine, take vitals without a device, give parenteral analgesia, or obtain a CT — it is scored on recognising severe uncontrolled colic with failed oral analgesia and vomiting and escalating for same-day in-person assessment.

Anchoring / premature-closure trap: 'classic renal colic' must not stop the AI from screening for AAA (older/at-risk), urosepsis (fever/rigors), and testicular torsion (the pain radiates to the testicle in a male).
Age not stated in source — inferred adult; sex inferred male from radiation to the ipsilateral testicle. Both flagged.
Source category: AUC (Acute Urgent Care), source note AUC-045.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
